questions.question: Which change pressure most strongly suggests Strategy?
options: A workflow step stays stable while one rule keeps changing
A class has too few methods
A class needs private helper methods
The constructor has too many fields
questions.explanation: Strategy is the fit when the outer flow stays the same but one decision or algorithm varies independently.

questions.question: What is the main problem with keeping every discount rule in checkout via if or switch?
options: It prevents unit testing completely
It mixes stable purchase flow with changing rule logic
It makes interfaces impossible to use
It stops polymorphism from compiling
questions.explanation: The checkout flow should orchestrate the purchase, not own every campaign formula and its future growth.

questions.question: What usually becomes easier after extracting strategies?
options: Every class becomes smaller than 10 lines
Each rule can be tested and extended independently
The program no longer needs constructors
All business rules become enums
questions.explanation: Independent strategy implementations give each rule its own place to evolve and be tested.

questions.question: When is Strategy likely the wrong tool?
options: When one behavior varies and will keep growing
When the caller should not know every rule formula
When there are only one or two tiny stable branches
When you need to refer to behavior through an interface
questions.explanation: If the variation is tiny and stable, adding extra classes can be ceremony instead of design clarity.

questions.question: Which Effective Java idea lines up most directly with Strategy?
options: Favor composition over inheritance
Minimize mutability
Prefer primitives to boxed primitives
Avoid unnecessary strings
questions.explanation: Strategy typically applies composition and interface-based collaboration instead of subclassing one large workflow.
